content:
Professional Summary # Results-oriented Software Engineer with a Master’s in Computer Engineering and extensive experience at Amazon Web Services (AWS). Proven track record in building cloud infrastructure from scratch, leading technical initiatives for high-scale container services (ECS/Fargate), and pioneering Agentic AI solutions to automate operational workflows.
Technical Skills # Languages: Java, Python, TypeScript Cloud & Infrastructure: AWS (EC2, ECS, Fargate, Bedrock, Lambda), Docker GenAI / ML: Agentic AI, AWS Bedrock, Strands Framework, Claude, Amazon Q Tools: Git, AWS CDK Experience # Amazon Web Services (AWS) — May 2019 – Present # Bedrock / Agentic AI
Architected a multi-agent solution using AWS Bedrock and the Strands framework to automate the “General Limit Increase” lifecycle — reduced end-to-end resolution time by 75% and eliminated 20 hours of weekly engineering overhead Pioneered a “solopreneur” workflow leveraging Claude and Amazon Q CLI to simulate a full development squad, accelerating the SDLC by 5x Built and shipped a production-grade codebase (12k+ lines, 100+ commits) in just 30 days Tech Lead, ECS Managed Instance Observability Service
Led a team of 7 to migrate from legacy WebSocket-based stateful systems to a stateless RESTful architecture, resolving a 100-connection-per-node bottleneck and mitigating 65k port exhaustion risks Engineered an Async I/O model achieving a 10x increase in single-node concurrency to support 200,000+ instances and 10k RPS peak throughput Developed a defense-in-depth authentication mechanism integrating IAM identity with VPC-source path validation Reduced manual overhead and improved on-call incident response efficiency by 80% via CloudWatch Cross-Account Observability Orchestrated end-to-end automation of Cell creation workflow, reducing delivery time from 6 months to 3 days (60x improvement) Bosch — 2015 – 2016 # Designed and developed a custom Android application for the Boost Recuperation System with Bluetooth/CAN protocol connectivity Developed a virtual testing environment for vehicle ECUs (ABS/ESP) Education # Degree School Year M.S. Computer Engineering New York University (NYU) 2017–2018 B.S. Computer Engineering Rose-Hulman Institute of Technology 2012–2015

title: About

content:
In a large-scale cloud environment, resources are ephemeral. When an instance is terminated, its internal IP is released back into the pool and often reassigned to a new instance within minutes. This creates a classic Race Condition in our mapping service.
The Problem: The “Stale Delete” # Imagine this scenario:
Instance A (IP: 10.0.0.1) is marked for deprovisioning. A “Delete” task is sent to the queue. The network jitters — the “Delete” task is delayed. Instance B is launched and gets the same IP: 10.0.0.1. It successfully registers itself in our DynamoDB mapping table. The delayed “Delete” task for Instance A finally arrives. Without a safeguard, the service would simply see a delete request for 10.0.0.1 and wipe out the new registration for Instance B. Instance B is now a “ghost” — it exists in the cell but is invisible to the control plane.
The Solution: Optimistic Locking with Timestamps # To solve this, we moved away from simple “Delete by Primary Key” and implemented Conditional Deletes.
We introduced a CreationTimestamp as a mandatory attribute for every mapping entry. When a deprovisioning workflow triggers a deletion, it must include the timestamp of the specific instance it intends to destroy.
The DynamoDB Logic # We leverage Condition Expressions to ensure atomicity. The deletion only executes if the timestamp in the database matches the timestamp in the request.
{ "TableName": "HostToCellMapping", "Key": { "HostIP": "10.0.0.1" }, "ConditionExpression": "CreationTimestamp = :expected_ts", "ExpressionAttributeValues": { ":expected_ts": "2024-03-07T10:00:00Z" } } This is a single atomic operation — no “Read-Then-Delete” round trip, no window for a race condition between the read and the write.
Handling the “Conflict” # If Instance B has already taken over the IP, the CreationTimestamp will not match, and DynamoDB will throw a ConditionalCheckFailedException.
Instead of treating this as a failure, our service interprets this as a “Logical Success.” It means the stale record is already gone, and we successfully avoided corrupting the new instance’s data. This “silent ignore” strategy prevents unnecessary alerts and keeps our system’s noise level low.
Why It Matters # Edge-Case Thinking — Not just building a CRUD service, but reasoning about failure modes at scale. Cost & Performance — A single atomic operation beats a “Read-Then-Delete” approach in both latency and cost. Alarm Fatigue — Distinguishing between a true error and a logical success keeps on-call noise low. A ConditionalCheckFailedException here is not a bug; it’s the system working correctly.

title: The "Ghost" in the Machine — Solving IP Reuse
